---
title: "Your Paper Title"
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
bibliography: references-appendix.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header: preamble-appendix.tex
---

This Internet Appendix supplements the main paper with the formal
derivation of Newton's law of cooling from first principles, a
discussion of how the cooling constant depends on the
heat-transfer coefficient and the geometry of the mug, and an
extension to the case of time-varying ambient temperature.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# Derivation of Newton's Law from First Principles {#sec-app-derivation}

This appendix derives the cooling law @eq-newton in the main
paper from a heat-balance argument and discusses the parameters
that determine the cooling constant $k$.

## The Heat-Balance Equation {#sec-app-derivation-balance}

Let $C$ denote the heat capacity of the coffee (J / $^\circ$C),
$h$ the heat-transfer coefficient at the mug-air interface
(W / m$^{2}$ / $^\circ$C), and $A$ the surface area of the mug
(m$^{2}$). The rate at which the coffee loses thermal energy is

$$
C\, \frac{dT}{dt} = -h\, A\, \bigl(T - T_\infty\bigr).
$$ {#eq-app-heatbalance}

Dividing both sides by $C$ gives the form of @eq-newton with

$$
k = \frac{h\, A}{C}.
$$ {#eq-app-kdef}

This identifies the cooling constant as the product of three
physical quantities. Holding $h$ and $C$ fixed, doubling the
surface area $A$ doubles $k$, which is why a wide shallow bowl
of coffee cools much faster than a tall narrow mug.

# The Cooling Constant and Mug Geometry {#sec-app-geometry}

For a cylindrical mug of radius $r$ and height $H$, the relevant
surface area for heat exchange with the surrounding air is the
top opening plus the lateral wall (the bottom usually sits on an
insulating surface),

$$
A = \pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r H,
$$ {#eq-app-area}

and the heat capacity scales with volume,

$$
C = \rho c_p\, \pi r^{2} H,
$$ {#eq-app-capacity}

where $\rho$ is the density and $c_p$ the specific heat of
coffee (approximately those of water). Substituting into
@eq-app-kdef gives

$$
k = \frac{h\, (\pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r H)}{\rho c_p\, \pi r^{2} H}
  = \frac{h}{\rho c_p}\left(\frac{1}{H} + \frac{2}{r}\right).
$$ {#eq-app-kgeom}

@eq-app-kgeom predicts that, holding the heat-transfer coefficient
fixed, $k$ decreases with both height and radius, and that the
two enter symmetrically only when $r = 2H$.

# Time-Varying Ambient Temperature {#sec-app-tvarying}

When the ambient temperature itself depends on time, $T_\infty
\to T_\infty(t)$, the cooling equation becomes

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = -k\bigl(T(t) - T_\infty(t)\bigr).
$$ {#eq-app-tvarying}

@eq-app-tvarying is a linear first-order ODE with an integrating
factor $e^{k t}$, and its general solution is

$$
T(t) = e^{-k t}\, T(0) + k \int_{0}^{t} e^{-k (t - s)}\, T_\infty(s)\, ds.
$$ {#eq-app-tvarying-soln}

The coffee temperature is an exponentially weighted moving
average of past ambient temperatures, with the weight given by
the cooling constant $k$.

::: {.theorem}
*(Long-run limit.)* If $T_\infty(t) \to \bar T$ as $t \to \infty$,
then $T(t) \to \bar T$ as $t \to \infty$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Apply the integrating-factor solution @eq-app-tvarying-soln and
take limits: the first term decays exponentially, and the
convolution term converges to $\bar T$ by dominated convergence.
$\square$
:::

# Notes on the Heat-Transfer Coefficient {#sec-app-htc}

The heat-transfer coefficient $h$ depends on whether the cooling
proceeds by natural convection, by forced convection (e.g. an
ambient draft), or by radiation; standard texts on transport
phenomena tabulate empirical correlations for each regime. The
results reported in @cooling-handbook2024 collect the relevant
constants for typical kitchen conditions and are consistent
with the magnitude of the $\widehat{k}$ estimated in the main
paper.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
